coredata.dc:title: One pot synthesis of C-dots and study on its interaction with nano ZnO through fluorescence quenching
coredata.dc:creator: Bharathi, D.
Krishna, R. Hari
Singh, Vikram
Kottam, Nagaraju
Siddlingeshwar, B.
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel fluorescent nanomaterials and elegant building blocks for future nano devices, and have garnered much interest as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots. Herein, we report the microwave assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots of size less than 10nm and the synthesis of Zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO) by combustion method. The synthesized ZnO nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscope and high resolution transmission electron microscope. Due to hazardous effect of ZnO nanoparticles on human health, its detection is important. The fluorescence quenching method has been employed to study the interaction between CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles involving steady state and time resolved measurements at room temperature. The interaction between CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles were studied, due to excellent overlap between the donors’ (CQD) emission spectrum and the acceptors’ (ZnO nanoparticles) absorption spectrum, Forster's resonance energy transfer theory is applied to measure the distance between the donor and acceptor (
r
0
= 5.56nm) as well as the critical energy transfer distance (
R
0
= 55.6Å).
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-quantum dots
Nano ZnO
Fluorescence quenching
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Energy transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of ZnO nanoparticles.

(a) Rietveld refinement of ZnO nanoparticles prepared using sugar as fuel Refined structure parameters along with the R-factor are summarized in Table 1. (b) Packing diagram of Wurtzite ZnO obtained using Rietveld parameters.
(a-c) Scanning electron micrographs of ZnO imaged at different magnifications.
(a–c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of ZnO HRTEM image of ZnO.
(a and b) Absorption and Emission spectra of CQDs respectively.
Fluorescence quenching of CQDs in the presence of ZnO nanoparticles.
Energy transfer mechanism for CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles.
Rietveld refined structural parameters for ZnO compound.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Crystal system = Hexagonal, Space group = P63 mc (No. 186).
Lattice parameters, a = 3.249(6) Å, c = 5.207(9) Å; Cell volume = 47.62(2) Å3.

head.ce:title: One pot synthesis of C-dots and study on its interaction with nano ZnO through fluorescence quenching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, M. S. Ramaiah Institute of Technology, Bengaluru 560054, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, M. S. Ramaiah Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, M. S. Ramaiah Institute of Technology, Bengaluru 560054, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, M. S. Ramaiah Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600 036, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel fluorescent nanomaterials and elegant building blocks for future nano devices, and have garnered much interest as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots. Herein, we report the microwave assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots of size less than 10nm and the synthesis of Zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO) by combustion method. The synthesized ZnO nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscope and high resolution transmission electron microscope. Due to hazardous effect of ZnO nanoparticles on human health, its detection is important. The fluorescence quenching method has been employed to study the interaction between CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles involving steady state and time resolved measurements at room temperature. The interaction between CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles were studied, due to excellent overlap between the donors’ (CQD) emission spectrum and the acceptors’ (ZnO nanoparticles) absorption spectrum, Forster's resonance energy transfer theory is applied to measure the distance between the donor and acceptor (
r
0
= 5.56nm) as well as the critical energy transfer distance (
R
0
= 55.6Å).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) are novel fluorescent materials with low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Due to their fascinating properties, CQDs are extensively studied amongst semiconductor quantum dots and may become elegant building blocks for future nano devices that have emerged recently. Less than 10nm sized CQDs have gained much importance due to their excellent photostability, low toxicity, favourable biocompatibility and excellent sensitivity to target analytes. CQDs have more advantages over conventional organic fluorophores since their optical properties are tuneable by material composition and size [1,2]. The optical properties have inspired many to study about carbon dots due to their unique potential for a wide variety of technical applications in biosensing, bioimaging and bio-tagging [3,4]. Numerous methods have been developed to synthesize carbon dots, including laser ablation [5], electrochemical Oxidation [6–8]. But these methods need complex treatment processes. Whereas other methods like hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave synthesis methods are widely accepted and involved due to their easy operating steps [9–13].

In recent years, many researchers have provided evidence that nanoparticles can reach the brain via blood-brain barrier (BBB) penetration or translocation along the olfactory nerve pathway and subsequently cause damage by the induction of oxidative stress, inflammatory responses, and cytotoxicity [14]. Many products such as beauty agents, toothpaste, sunscreens, and biomedicines contain ZnO nanoparticles. Because of their special properties including small size (porous) and high specific surface area, the biological safety of nanoparticles have received wide attention. Tian et al. [15] have focussed on neurotoxicity of ZnO nanoparticles that have been extensively studied in different animal systems and cell types. Amedea et al. [16] have discussed about the nanotoxicology of metal oxide nanoparticles. Wang et al. [17] have studied the effect of long-term exposure to ZnO nanoparticles and Bush et al. [18] have demonstrated about cascades of oxidative stress due to neurotoxicity of metallic nanoparticles leading to Alzheimer's disease. These studies motivated us to investigate the interaction between CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles. The present study would open up the possible usage of CQDs in sensing metal nanoparticles in particular for bioanalytical applications.

Critical literature survey reveals that attempt have not been made so far to investigate the interaction between CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles. In this present work, the fluorescence quenching and Forster's resonance energy transfer (FRET) techniques are employed to study the interactions. Wherein the Stern-Volmer constant, bimolecular quenching rate constant, energy transfer, critical energy and the distance between donor and the acceptor are determined.

Ascorbic acid vitamin C, Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), Poly ethylene glycol (PEG200), Zinc nitrate and Normal sugar were purchased from Merck. The CQDs are synthesized through microwave assisted method due to the various advantages that includes reduced reaction times, increased productivity, high quantum yield and ultimately enhanced efficiency of the technique. 1.5g of Ascorbic acid vitamin C is dissolved in 10mL distilled water, 0.06g of EDTA is added to the above solution and stirred thoroughly, to which 2mL Poly ethylene glycol PEG 200 is added, and the solution is stirred for 15min and heated in microwave oven for 1h, the obtained CQDs are cooled for 1h and 30mL of distilled water is added to it, and the synthesized CQDs are in liquid form, possessing high Quantum yield and no obvious crystal lattice.

ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized by combustion method, 5g of zinc nitrate and 1.2g of sugar is added to 50mL distilled water and stirred for half an hour. The mixture was introduced into preheated muffle furnace at 500°C. The solution undergoes dehydration and burns with evolution of gases. At the end of combustion porous white product of ZnO nanoparticles were obtained. Further, the colloidal ZnO required for energy transfer studies was prepared by dissolving 24mg of ZnO nanoparticles in 15mL distilled water and sonicated for 15min. Since the ZnO nanoparticles were porous, it is easily dispersible in aqueous medium.

The synthesized CQDs size is determined by using Malvern instrument, ZETASIZER Nano series. X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded in a Rigaku, Smartlab X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (
λ
= 0.154nm). The XRD pattern in the 2θ range of 20–80° are recorded at a scan rate of 0.05°min−1. FESEM was employed for morphological study of ZnO nanoparticles using the ULTRA 55 Field emission scanning electron microscope operated at 5kV. HRTEM was employed for particle size study of ZnO nanoparticles using JEOL JEM – 2100F operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV and the UV–Vis spectra of the nanoparticles is measured using Analytik Jena Specord 200 plus.

The absorption spectral measurements were recorded using STS-VIS miniature spectrometer and Elico SL 159 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurements were measured using HITACHI F-2700 spectrofluorimeter, the excitation and emission slit widths (each 5nm) and scan rate (1500nm/min) and PMT voltage (700V) were maintained constant for all the measurements.

The fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out using a Horiba Jobin Yvon TCSPC (time correlated single photon counting) lifetime instrument. The light source used was 370nm nano LED. 1MHz pulse repetition rate and 370nm LED with the instrumental full width at half-maximum is set, including ∼1.1ns detector response. The Scatterer (Ludox AS40 colloidal silica) is used to collect instrument response function. IBH software is employed to measure the decay rate. The symmetrical distribution of the residuals was analysed with a value of
χ
2
, 0.99 ≤
χ
2
≤ 1.2, to be considered as a good fit. By using the following equation the average fluorescence lifetime (
τ
avg
) values were obtained [19].
(1)
τ
avg
=
(
∑
i
=
1
n
α
i
τ
i
2
)
(
∑
i
=
1
n
α
i
τ
i
)
Where
τ
i
is the individual lifetime with corresponding amplitude
α
i
.

The PXRD of the combustion derived product obtained using sugar as fuel is shown in Fig. 1. XRD patterns of the sample show peaks corresponding to all the planes of standard wurtzite phase ZnO (JCPDS 70-0134). Diffraction patterns are found to be well resolved with appreciable peak intensity and are broad in nature. This nature of XRD peaks indicate that the obtained ZnO powder is crystalline and is in nano regime. The average crystallite size (D) of ZnO sample is estimated using Scherrer's formula [20] from the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the (100), (002) and (101) diffraction peaks, and is found to be ~17nm.

Further, the PXRD step scan data of ZnO obtained is analysed by Rietveld refinement to obtain detail structural parameters. The Rietveld refinement was performed using specially programmed software, the Full proof that can simultaneously refine structural and micro structural parameters. Structural parameters such as atomic positions, lattice constants and occupancy positions along with microstructural parameters viz. crystallite size and lattice strain can be accurately obtained. The mathematical procedures followed in Rietveld refinement are complicated and out of interest in present study. Therefore, brief procedure followed for refinement of ZnO data in present study is described. The refinement was carried out with P63 mc space group (No. 186). To begin with the errors such as zero-point shift, unit cell and the parameters related to the background were carried out. This is followed by refinement of peak profile parameters namely intensity, FWHM and location. To further improve the refinement few other parameters like occupancy, thermal parameter, lattice parameter and atomic positions were refined. The experimental profiles were fitted with the most suitable pseudo-Voigt analytical function [21]. The difference in observed and calculated diffraction patterns were minimized by following the Marquardt least- squares procedures. A good agreement between the experimental relative intensity and stimulated intensity was obtained as evident from Yobs˗Ycal in Fig. 2(a). Using the lattice parameters obtained from refinement, representative crystal structure has been generated and is shown in Fig. 2(b) ( Table 1).

Fig. 3(a-c) shows the scanning electron micrographs of ZnO imaged at different magnifications. From the low magnification SEM image (Fig. 3a) it is evident that the product shows porous morphology with numerous voids of different sizes. The decomposition of the precursors into CO2, H2O that are evolved as gaseous by products result in pores and voids in the structure. Careful observation of high magnification SEM images (Figs. 3b and 3c) reveals that the particles are near spherical and interconnected to each other forming a network of agglomerated clusters. The particles are found to be in varying size due to the difference in the distribution of heat during combustion. Porous structure with voids and agglomerated particles are typical of combustion derived products and is consistent with the available literature [22,23].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of ZnO are shown in Fig. 4(a–c). TEM image (Fig. 4a) shows near spherical particles agglomerated having average diameter ~ 20nm. It can be noticed that a few elongated particles are also present along with near spherical ones. The formation of elongated particles might be due to local sintering during high temperature achieved in combustion synthesis. The six closed concentric rings seen in SAED pattern (Fig. 4b) reveal polycrystalline nature. The spotty closed rings are indexed according to ZnO wurtzite structure from the d spacing values. The interplanar distance of 0.27nm observed in the HRTEM lattice fringe is comparable to the interplanar distance of the (002) plane in ZnO.

UV–Visible diffused reflectance spectroscopy is one of the standard techniques for the measurement of the optical band gap (Eg) of the powdered samples. The optical band gap of the as prepared and the calcined samples at different temperatures is estimated by using Kubelka-Munk function. In order to study the band gap of ZnO nanoparticles, the intercept of the tangents in the plots of [F (R∞) hυ] 1/2 versus photon energy (hυ) is plotted (Fig. S1) (ESI#). The Kubelka–Munk function [F (R∞)] and photon energy (hυ) can be calculated by following equations.
(2)
F
(
R
∞
)
=
(
1 -
R
∞
)
2
/ 2
R
∞
(3)
R
∞
=
10
- A
(4)
h υ
=
1240 / λ
Where, R∞ is the reflection coefficient of the sample, A is the absorbance intensity of sample and λ is the wavelength of absorption [24]. From the intercept of the extrapolated linear portion, the Eg values of as formed ZnO nanophosphor is found to be 3.2eV. The obtained Eg values are in good agreement with the literature values [22].

The particle size of synthesized CQDs is 6nm (pH 3) determined through ZETASIZER Nano series instrument. Fig. S2 (ESI#) shows the particle size distribution of the CQDs by volume. We have also measured the size of CQDs at different pH values and found that the size was less than 10nm for all pH values. The size of CQDs decreases with increase in pH till pH 6 and remains unaltered further from pH 7–12. In addition, the zeta potentials of CQDs at various pH values were measured. The zeta potential of CQDs was measured positive at pH 3 and negative for all other higher pH values.

To investigate the optical properties of CQDs, the absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured. Fig. 5(a) displays the typical absorption spectra recorded for the CQDs, we notice that the spectrum is broad ranging from 290 to 350nm. We have also tried and found the most reported feature of fluorescent carbon dots emission wavelength dependence on the excitation wavelength. In our case the fluorescence spectra of CQDs have multi-emission nature depending on different excitation wavelength. The maximum fluorescence emission peak was measured at 425nm for an excitation wavelength of 340nm. It is noticed that the increase in excitation wavelength caused a red shift in the fluorescence emission peak. The typical fluorescence emission peak of CQDs at 425nm with optimal excitation wavelength of 340nm is shown in Fig. 5(b).

CQDs were synthesized via microwave assisted routes in aqueous solution through a straightforward one-pot process using safe and low-cost inorganic salts as precursors. Fluorescence quenching is one of the best methods used for the sensor based applications. We have used CQDs as fluorophore and ZnO nanoparticles as quencher. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs is analysed by using Stern-Volmer relation:
(5)
I
o
I
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
= 1 +
k
q
τ
o
[ Q ]
Where
I
0
and
I
are the emission intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of ZnO nanoparticles respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, [Q] is the concentration of ZnO nanoparticles, kq is the bimolecular quenching rate constant and
τ
0
is the excited state lifetime of CQDs.

τ
0
is measured to be 2ns by employing tri-exponentially fitting parameter using TCSPC (Time correlated single photon counting) instrument. Eq. (5) is considerable as long as the experimental results show linear variation. The typical Stern-Volmer plot for CQDs with ZnO nanoparticles is shown in Fig. S3(ESI#). We obtained a linear curve for the plot of I0/I versus [ZnO]. From Stern-Volmer plot it is observed that I0/I is proportional to [ZnO] with correlation coefficient of 1 in the concentration range between 0 and 20mM with the detection limit of 4mM. Further, the S-V plot begins to show positive deviation from linearity, as the concentration of ZnO nanoparticles increases. The CQDs fluorescence spectra were recorded in the presence of ZnO concentration ranging from 4mM to 20mM. From Fig. 6 it is observed that the CQDs fluorescence intensity is decreased by the addition of ZnO nanoparticles. There is a slight decrease in the intensity of the scattering band at 340nm. It is worth noticing that the peak at ~340nm also decreases with increase in the concentration of ZnO. In order to understand this, similar experiments were carried out without CQDs (Figure not shown) and the results showed that, even in the absence of CQDs the peak at ~340nm decreases. This result suggests that there is scattering of part of exciting photons which also results in the decrease of CQDs’ emission. Hence, it should be understood that along with the quenching effect, scattering effect also contributes for the observed decrease of C-dots emission.

By using linear fit method, from Fig. S3(ESI#) we have calculated the Stern-Volmer constant Ksv = 25M−1, and the bimolecular quenching rate parameter
kq
=1.23×1010 M−1s−1. The obtained
kq
value is ≈ 1×1010 M−1 s−1, which indicates that the quenching process is diffusion limited. Thus, the detection of zinc in CQDs is diffusion limited. The plot of
τ
0
/
τ
vs [ZnO] is shown in Fig. S3(ESI#). Where
τ
0
is the lifetime of CQDs in the absence and
τ
is the lifetime of CQDs in the presence of quencher ZnO. The process of quenching follows the Stern-Volmer relation in case of time resolved method. The linearity in the curve again confirms the dynamic quenching mechanism [25–27]. Though the change of lifetime is very minimal (less than 10%), which is touching the boundary of instrument (limited to instrumental resolution), the lifetime change is not visible by eye. The change in I0/I and τ0/τ is almost same with ZnO concentration which suggest the quenching to be dynamic. The value of Ksv (in the range of 10–100M−1) and Kq is also supporting towards dynamic quenching.

The CQDs quantum yield
(
∅
f
)
is measured by comparison method using following Eq. (6) [28].
(6)
∅
f
=
∅
r
(
I
f
A
r
n
f
2
)
(
I
r
A
f
n
r
2
)
Where the subscript f and r refers to the CQDs and the reference molecules,
∅
f
is the quantum yield of CQDs,
∅
r
is the known quantum yield, I
f is the integral intensity of CQDs,
A
f
is the area under the emission curve of CQDs, the refractive index of the solvents used is represented as
n
f
and
n
r
for CQDs and the reference molecules respectively. The measured quantum yield of CQDs is 42%, using the refractive index
n
f
= 1.33 (water) and
n
r
= 1.497(toluene). (Z)-N, N-diethyl-N′-(7-oxo-7H-benzo[de]anthracen-3-yl) acetamidine in toluene (
∅
r
= 0.56) is used as the reference molecule [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Non-radiative energy transfer process from a fluorescent donor to a lower energy acceptor is defined as Forster's resonance energy transfer (FRET) through long-range dipole-dipole interactions [19]. Fluorescent CQDs have played a significant role in sensor based applications using FRET technologies, where FRET is used to measure changes in biomolecular conformations [30–33]. CQDs acts as an efficient energy transfer donors in FRET based applications [34,35]. Any fluorescent moiety, including fluorescent proteins, organic small-molecule dyes, lanthanide dyes and fluorescent nanoparticles can be used as donors and either fluorophores or Quenchers (non-fluorescent energy acceptors) can be used as acceptors [19,36]. Some of the organic molecules and metal nanoparticles also play the role of an acceptor.

By considering the excitation wavelength of CQDs, the excited state energy is calculated by using the equation, E=hν, where ‘E’ is CQDs excited state energy, ‘h’ is the Planck's constant and ‘ν’ = c/λ, where ‘c’ is speed of light and ‘λ’ is the CQDs excitation wavelength. The calculated energy value of CQDs is 3.7eV and the band gap energy calculated for ZnO nanoparticles is 3.2eV therefore the energy of CQDs is greater compared to ZnO nanoparticles and the energy transfer from CQDs to ZnO cannot be ruled out. The decrease in fluorescence intensity is thus attributed to the energy transfer between CQDs and the ZnO nanoparticles. The energy transfer mechanism involved in CQDs/ZnO is as shown in Fig. 7.

In order to demonstrate the feasibility of CQDs/ZnO FRET system in the detection of zinc, the fluorescence and absorption spectra of CQDs and ZnO were investigated and are shown in Fig. S4(ESI#). There is an evident overlap between the absorption spectrum of ZnO and emission spectrum of CQDs which is essential for efficient FRET process.

In order to understand the FRET process in detail, we tried to obtain the efficiency of energy transfer (E) by the following Eq. (7).
(7)
E = 1 −
I
I
0
Where,
I
0
is the emission intensity of the donor (CQDs) alone and I is the emission intensity of donor (CQDs) in the presence of acceptor (ZnO). Fig. S5(ESI#) shows the energy transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanoparticles at different ZnO concentration range. It is observed that the energy transfer is 50%. The other factors on which FRET depends are the spectral overlap between the donors’ emission spectrum and the acceptors’ absorption spectrum, and also depends on the Forster's distance value that should range from 20 to 60Å at which FRET is 50% efficient and the donor-acceptor distance must be smaller than 10nm [19,37,38]. The relation between energy transfer (E), donor- acceptor distance (
r
o
) and the critical energy transfer distance (
R
0
) when the transfer efficiency is 50% is given below.
(8)
E
=
R
0
6
R
0
6
+
r
0
6

The degree of spectral overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption is represented by the overlap integral J (
λ
) the value of J (
λ
) can be calculated by using Eq. (9),
(9)
J ( λ )
=
∫
0
∞
F
D
( λ
ε
A
(
λ
)
λ
4
d λ
Where
F
D
(
λ
) is the fluorescence intensity with respect to the corrected emission spectrum with its area normalized to unity
,
ε
A
(
λ
)
is the acceptor molar absorptivity.

If J (
λ
) is in units of M
−1 cm3 and the wavelength is expressed in cm, then the Forster's distance can be obtained [19],
(10)
R
0
6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 25
(
κ
2
n
− 4
ϕ
D
J ( λ ) )
( in
c
m
6
)

In Eq. (10), κ 2 is often assumed to be 2/3 as this value represents isotropically oriented dipoles or spectral orientation factor of the dipole for freely rotating donor and acceptor molecules, n is the refractive index of the medium,
ϕ
D
is the donor (CQDs) fluorescence Quantum yield and J (
λ
) is the spectral overlap integral of the absorption of the acceptor (ZnO) and emission of the donor (CQDs).

The spectral overlap integral value J (
λ
) is calculated to be 5.669×10–13 M−1 cm3, using excitation wavelength of 340nm in Eq. (9). The energy transfer efficiency (E) is calculated to be 0.5 using Eq. (7) in which 50% energy transfer from CQDs is possible. The critical distance
R
0
= 55.6Å from Eq. (8) is calculated using
κ
2
= 2/3, n = 1.33 and
ϕ
D
= 0.42. For the present system, the calculated value of
R
0
is in the range of maximal critical distance (20–60Å). Therefore it is in accordance with the conditions of FRET method [19]. On using E and
R
0
in the Eq. (8) the donor-acceptor distance value obtained is
r
0
= 5.56nm which is in accordance with the FRET method suggesting high probability for energy transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanoparticles. From the above discussion, it can be concluded that FRET probe was established, in which CQDs was selected as donor and ZnO as acceptor.

The water dispersible CQDs of particle size ~6nm were prepared through microwave approach and ZnO nanoparticles were prepared by combustion method. Fluorescence quenching technique is employed to study the interaction between nano ZnO and CQDs. The S-V constant and the bimolecular quenching rate constant values indicates the detection of zinc in the present system is diffusion limited and quenching is dynamic in nature. By comparison method the Quantum yield of CQDs obtained was 42%. The Forster's critical distance R0 value was measured to be 55.6Å for 50% energy transfer, with donor-acceptor distance ro=5.56nm that was in accordance with the predicted value for FRET method. The energy transfer between CQDs (donor) and ZnO (acceptor) were confirmed by the FRET studies. The proposed method is thus expected to become a potential tool for the fast response of zinc.
All additional figures (Figs. S1–S5) are given in the supporting information.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank Prof. A K Mishra, Dept. of Chemistry, IITM Chennai for providing TCSPC facilities for this work. Authors also thank Dr. C Shivakumara, SSCU, IISc Bangalore for help in Rietveld refinement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.05.077.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemistry of Nanocrystalline Oxide Materials: Combustion Synthesis Properties and Applications
How to Apply FRET: From Experimental Design to Data Analysis. In FRET—Förster Resonance Energy Transfer
